rules.description: Consumer Rights Act statutory information
Pricing transparency: total price including unavoidable fees
Subscription traders must send reminders before renewal and offer one-click cancellation.
Customer-facing websites must offer reasonable adjustments under the Equality Act 2010.
Identity of the controller must be disclosed on first data collection page
Contact details of the controller (including DPO if applicable)
Purposes of processing AND legal basis
International transfers and safeguards (SCCs / adequacy)
Retention period or criteria
Right to access, rectify, erase, restrict, port, object
Right to withdraw consent (if consent is the basis)
Right to complain to ICO
Analytics cookies require consent (ICO position 2023)
Cookie banner must not use pre-ticked boxes
Cookie wall (pay-or-OK) for unrelated content is not freely-given consent
Soft opt-in: prior similar product/service customer relationship for marketing
Marketing emails must include sender identity (no false header)
Marketing emails must include valid return address
Easy opt-out mechanism in every marketing email
Cookie consent before non-essential cookies set
Cookie reject must be as prominent as accept
Cookie purpose disclosed clearly before consent
Business identification on website (Co. No, VAT, Address)
Right-to-know disclosure on data collected
Right to delete disclosure
Right to opt out of sale or sharing
Do Not Sell or Share My Personal Information link
California sites must offer Limit Use of Sensitive Personal Information opt-out link.
Subject line cannot be deceptive
From header must identify sender
Postal address required in email
Opt-out within 10 business days
Calls/texts to US numbers require prior express written consent; AI voice = artificial voice.
Author byline on substantive content (Experience + Expertise)
Author credentials visible (Authoritativeness)
Honest reviews disclosure (no fake or paid reviews)
All-in pricing, no hidden mandatory fees (DMCC drip-pricing ban)
UK limited companies must display registered name, number, registered office on every website.
Consumer Rights Act 2015 requires clear rights notice on commercial websites.
Total price including mandatory fees must be displayed up-front, not added at checkout.
Must publish a policy declaring no incentivised, fake or undisclosed reviews.
Reviews or testimonials shown without disclosing incentives or a genuine-review verification process
Clear subscription terms + easy cancellation (DMCC)
Section 3 personal data definition disclosure
Legitimate interests (if relied on) clearly stated
Recipients or categories of recipients of personal data
Whether provision is statutory/contractual and consequences of not providing
Existence of automated decision-making including profiling
Health and Safety policy disclosure
Cookie purposes must be itemised (functional vs analytics vs marketing)
Cookie list must include third-party cookies
Cookie banner copy must be available before clicking accept
Cookie consent log must be retained for 12 months minimum
Re-prompt for consent after material changes to cookie usage
Commercial organisations turnover >£36M must publish annual Modern Slavery Act statement.
Marketing or analytics trackers present but no consent-management platform gating them before consent
Cookie banner must offer a Reject-All / non-essential-refusal option as prominent and functional as Accept-All
B2B corporate subscriber: soft opt-in still required for individual employees identifiable in role
Cookie withdrawal mechanism accessible from every page
Unit pricing for pre-packaged goods
US public-accommodation website with no evident accessibility conformance under ADA Title III
WCAG 2.1 AA accessibility statement
Personal data sold or shared for advertising with no Do Not Sell or Share opt-out link
Commercial messages must be labelled if subscriber opted in for transactional only
Reviews or testimonials shown to US consumers without genuine-review safeguards under the FTC Consumer Review Rule
Sites with influencer, affiliate or AI content must disclose endorsements clearly.
"Do Not Sell or Share" / "Your Privacy Choices" opt-out link
Honour the Global Privacy Control universal opt-out signal
State-compliant privacy notice (categories, purposes, rights, opt-out methods)
One-to-one prior express written consent for calls/texts (TCPA/FCC)
Texas requires a specific sensitive-data notice on sites that process biometric or sensitive data.
Virginia requires a controller privacy notice including access, deletion, portability rights.
Citations to authoritative sources (Trust)
Published content shows author / expertise signals (E-E-A-T)
Incorporated entity not displaying company number, registered office and place of registration on its website
VAT-registered businesses must show VAT number on website per HMRC guidance.
Online sales with no visible 14-day cancellation, returns or refund rights for distance consumers
Fair, transparent and legible consumer contract terms
Age-appropriate design (ICO Children Code)
Section 22 manual unstructured data exemption noted where applicable
Image alt text as a reasonable adjustment
UK right of access (Subject Access Request)
UK right to erasure
UK right to data portability
UK right to object to direct marketing
UK processor / sub-processor transparency
UK records of processing (ROPA)
UK lawful basis identified
RIDDOR reportable incident reference
Cookie scan must distinguish session vs persistent cookies
Cookie banner not shown to authenticated users with stored preferences
Valid marketing consent or soft opt-in for email/SMS
Strictly necessary cookies do not require consent but must be disclosed
Distance-selling pre-contract information (CCRs 2013)
Accessibility statement + WCAG 2.1 AA (ADA Title III)
"Limit the Use of My Sensitive Personal Information" link
Endorsements, ambassador or affiliate content without a clear material-connection disclosure
Consumer rights (access, delete, correct, opt-out, appeal) + request method
Opt-in / limit for sensitive data (Nebraska, Maryland MODPA, others)
SMS or text marketing collected without prior express written consent and required disclosures
Texas opt-out of sale + sensitive-data consent
VAT number displayed where VAT-registered
Organisation processing personal data with no sign of ICO data-protection-fee registration
Accurate health-and-safety / competence claims
Modern Slavery statement (turnover GBP 36M+)
Clear price marking incl. unit/total price
Virginia consumer rights + mandatory appeal process

pointers.layman_explanation: Under the Consumer Rights Act 2015 and Consumer Contracts Regulations 2013 you must give consumers clear pre-contract information (trader identity, total price, the main service characteristics, and cancellation rights) before they commit. Missing this makes contracts harder to enforce and draws Trading Standards action.
pointers.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia adds a compliant pre-contract information block: trader identity, total price, key service terms and the consumer cancellation right.
pointers.recommendation: Tamazia adds a compliant pre-contract information block: trader identity, total price, key service terms and the consumer cancellation right.
pointers.enforcement_example: CMA under the DMCC Act 2024: direct consumer-law fines up to 10% of global turnover plus personal penalties up to GBP 300,000.

bingo.problem: Right now: Consumer Rights Act statutory information.
bingo.fix: Tamazia adds a compliant pre-contract information block: trader identity, total price, key service terms and the consumer cancellation right.

pointers.fact: Pricing transparency: total price including unavoidable fees
pointers.layman_explanation: CMA Digital Markets, Competition and Consumers Act 2024 requires you to show total prices without drip-pricing. The CMA has powers to fine 10% of global turnover.
pointers.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia redesigns the pricing flow to show total price upfront and removes drip-pricing patterns.
pointers.recommendation: Tamazia redesigns the pricing flow to show total price upfront and removes drip-pricing patterns.
pointers.enforcement_example: CMA under the DMCC Act 2024: direct consumer-law fines up to 10% of global turnover plus personal penalties up to GBP 300,000.

bingo.problem: Right now: Pricing transparency: total price including unavoidable fees.
bingo.fix: Tamazia redesigns the pricing flow to show total price upfront and removes drip-pricing patterns.

pointers.fact: Identity of the controller must be disclosed on first data collection page
pointers.layman_explanation: Your privacy notice does not name who the legal "data controller" is. Visitors cannot tell who is collecting their data. ICO treats this as a first-mover breach of Article 13(1)(a) and opens an inquiry within weeks.
pointers.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia drafts the controller-identity disclosure block (registered name, company number, registered office, ICO registration) and inserts it as paragraph 1 of the privacy notice.
pointers.recommendation: Tamazia drafts the controller-identity disclosure block (registered name, company number, registered office, ICO registration) and inserts it as paragraph 1 of the privacy notice.
pointers.enforcement_example: ICO v doorstep canvasser 2023: £130,000 for failing to identify controller

bingo.problem: Right now: Identity of the controller must be disclosed on first data collection page.
bingo.fix: Tamazia drafts the controller-identity disclosure block (registered name, company number, registered office, ICO registration) and inserts it as paragraph 1 of the privacy notice.

pointers.fact: Right to access, rectify, erase, restrict, port, object
pointers.layman_explanation: Visitors cannot find their statutory rights: access, rectification, erasure, restriction, portability, objection. Article 13(2)(b) requires you to enumerate them and explain how to exercise each.
pointers.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia drafts the data-subject-rights section (one paragraph per right) plus the rights request workflow (email + verification flow).
pointers.recommendation: Tamazia drafts the data-subject-rights section (one paragraph per right) plus the rights request workflow (email + verification flow).

bingo.problem: Right now: Right to access, rectify, erase, restrict, port, object.
bingo.fix: Tamazia drafts the data-subject-rights section (one paragraph per right) plus the rights request workflow (email + verification flow).

pointers.fact: Business identification on website (Co. No, VAT, Address)
pointers.layman_explanation: Business identification on website (Co. No, VAT, Address). This is a specific requirement of UK TRADING STANDARDS, which the relevant regulator enforces; for a firm selling trust, its absence on the live site is an avoidable compliance and credibility breach.
pointers.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia implements and verifies "Business identification on website (Co. No, VAT, Address)" on your site so it satisfies UK TRADING STANDARDS.
pointers.recommendation: Tamazia implements and verifies "Business identification on website (Co. No, VAT, Address)" on your site so it satisfies UK TRADING STANDARDS.
pointers.enforcement_example: Trading Standards: enforcement orders and prosecution with unlimited fines on conviction.

bingo.fix: Tamazia implements and verifies "Business identification on website (Co. No, VAT, Address)" on your site so it satisfies UK TRADING STANDARDS.

pointers.fact: Honest reviews disclosure (no fake or paid reviews)
pointers.layman_explanation: The DMCC Act 2024 bans fake and undisclosed-incentivised reviews and the CMA can fine up to 10 percent of global turnover. If you display reviews or testimonials you must be able to show they are genuine and disclose any incentive.
pointers.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia adds review verification and incentive disclosure, and removes any testimonial that cannot be substantiated.
pointers.recommendation: Tamazia adds review verification and incentive disclosure, and removes any testimonial that cannot be substantiated.

pointers.fact: No Organization or LocalBusiness schema in your structured data.
pointers.layman_explanation: AI engines (ChatGPT, Claude, Perplexity, Google AI) read Schema.org to know who you are. With no Organization entity they cannot reliably identify, trust or cite your firm when a buyer asks for a provider in your field.
pointers.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia ships full Organization/LocalBusiness schema with name, logo, sameAs and contact points.
pointers.recommendation: Tamazia ships full Organization/LocalBusiness schema with name, logo, sameAs and contact points.
pointers.evidence: homepage JSON-LD · no Organization type detected

bingo.problem: Right now: No Organization or LocalBusiness schema in your structured data.
bingo.fix: Tamazia ships full Organization/LocalBusiness schema with name, logo, sameAs and contact points.

pointers.fact: Your AI entity-readiness score is 57/100: no Organization entity schema; no sameAs links to your authoritative profiles; no Wikidata knowledge-graph entry; no llms.txt.
pointers.layman_explanation: AI answer engines decide who to cite by first identifying you as a known, trusted entity. They read your Organization schema, the sameAs links that connect you to your verified profiles, and knowledge bases like Wikidata. You are missing 4 of these anchors, so to an AI engine you are an unknown string rather than a citable provider, and it names the firms it can identify instead.
pointers.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia builds your machine-readable entity: full Organization schema with sameAs, a Wikidata entry, and an llms.txt, so the answer engines can identify and cite you.
pointers.evidence_quote: no Organization entity schema; no sameAs links to your authoritative profiles; no Wikidata knowledge-graph entry; no llms.txt

bingo.fix: Tamazia builds your machine-readable entity: full Organization schema with sameAs, a Wikidata entry, and an llms.txt, so the answer engines can identify and cite you.

pointers.recommendation: Tamazia expands the homepage and key service pages with substantive, E-E-A-T-rich content targeting your buyer queries.

pointers.fact: For "best luxury fitness clubs online" you are not visible in the top 100; luxurylondon.co.uk owns position #1.
pointers.layman_explanation: When your buyers search "best luxury fitness clubs online", they find luxurylondon.co.uk, not you. Page two and below is effectively zero clicks, so this exact query hands its high-intent traffic to a competitor every day, and AI answers cite the top-ranked pages, so being invisible here also keeps you out of the AI results. The firms winning your buyer queries: luxurylondon.co.uk, fitnessfirst.co.uk, ibtimes.co.uk.
pointers.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia builds the page depth and authority to move "best luxury fitness clubs online" into the top three, where the clicks and the AI citations are.
pointers.evidence_quote: live SERP: you not visible in the top 100 vs luxurylondon.co.uk #1
pointers.evidence: live SERP (free-serp) · query "best luxury fitness clubs online"

bingo.fix: Tamazia builds the page depth and authority to move "best luxury fitness clubs online" into the top three, where the clicks and the AI citations are.

pointers.fact: Incorporated entity not displaying company number, registered office and place of registration on its website
pointers.layman_explanation: A UK limited company must print its company number, where it is registered and its registered office on the website. Many firms only put the trading name, which is an offence.
pointers.tamazia_fix_short: Add the full trading-disclosure block (registered name, number, place of registration, registered office) to the footer or contact page.
pointers.recommendation: Add the full trading-disclosure block (registered name, number, place of registration, registered office) to the footer or contact page.
pointers.evidence_quote: Unlimited Signature Classes, precision-backed Personal Training, and exclusive amenities to recover and regenerate.
pointers.enforcement_example: The Companies (Trading Disclosures) Regulations 2015 and Companies Act 2006 s82 require a company to show its registered name, number, place of registration and registered office address on its website. Failure is an offence carrying a 1000 pound fine plus 100 pounds a day.

bingo.problem: Right now: Incorporated entity not displaying company number, registered office and place of registration on its website. Here it is on your site: “Unlimited Signature Classes, precision-backed Personal Training, and exclusive amenities to recover and regenerate.”.
bingo.fix: Tamazia add the full trading-disclosure block (registered name, number, place of registration, registered office) to the footer or contact page.

pointers.fact: Online sales with no visible 14-day cancellation, returns or refund rights for distance consumers
pointers.layman_explanation: Selling online to UK consumers means you must tell them about their 14-day right to cancel and your returns and refund policy before they buy.
pointers.tamazia_fix_short: Publish clear cancellation, returns and refund terms and surface them at checkout.
pointers.recommendation: Publish clear cancellation, returns and refund terms and surface them at checkout.
pointers.evidence_quote: You can opt out of marketing or advertising emails by using the “unsubscribe” link in the email at any time.
pointers.enforcement_example: The Consumer Contracts Regulations 2013 give distance-selling consumers a 14-day cancellation right that must be communicated; the Consumer Rights Act 2015 governs refunds. Trading Standards enforce and the DMCC raises consumer-law fines to 10 percent of turnover.

pointers.recommendation: Tamazia removes unused CSS.

pointers.layman_explanation: by reducing the length of chains, reducing the download size of resources, or deferring the download of unnecessary resources to improve page load. Measured live by Google PageSpeed Insights on your mobile site.

pointers.recommendation: Tamazia flattens the network dependency chain.

pointers.fact: No Service or Offer schema describing what you sell.
pointers.layman_explanation: AI engines extract Service/Offer schema to answer "who offers X". Without it your services are not machine-readable and you are left out of those answers.
pointers.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia marks up each service with Service schema and clear offers.
pointers.recommendation: Tamazia marks up each service with Service schema and clear offers.

bingo.problem: Right now: No Service or Offer schema describing what you sell.
bingo.fix: Tamazia marks up each service with Service schema and clear offers.

pointers.fact: No FAQPage schema or structured Q&A.
pointers.layman_explanation: Answer engines preferentially quote structured Q&A. With no FAQPage you miss the single format LLMs cite most when answering buyer questions.
pointers.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia builds an FAQ programme with FAQPage schema targeting real buyer questions.
pointers.recommendation: Tamazia builds an FAQ programme with FAQPage schema targeting real buyer questions.

bingo.problem: Right now: No FAQPage schema or structured Q&A.
bingo.fix: Tamazia builds an FAQ programme with FAQPage schema targeting real buyer questions.

pointers.layman_explanation: Star ratings shown in AI and search answers come from Review/AggregateRating schema. Without it your reputation is invisible to the engines buyers now ask first.
pointers.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia adds compliant Review/AggregateRating schema (DMCC-safe, genuine reviews only).
pointers.recommendation: Tamazia adds compliant Review/AggregateRating schema (DMCC-safe, genuine reviews only).

bingo.problem: Right now: No Review or AggregateRating schema.
bingo.fix: Tamazia adds compliant Review/AggregateRating schema (DMCC-safe, genuine reviews only).

pointers.layman_explanation: Breadcrumb schema helps search and AI understand your site structure and surface deep service pages, not just the homepage.
pointers.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia adds BreadcrumbList schema sitewide.
pointers.recommendation: Tamazia adds BreadcrumbList schema sitewide.

bingo.problem: Right now: No BreadcrumbList schema.
bingo.fix: Tamazia adds BreadcrumbList schema sitewide.

pointers.fact: No bylined author or Person schema establishing expertise.
pointers.layman_explanation: Google E-E-A-T and LLM trust models reward demonstrable Experience, Expertise, Authority and Trust. Anonymous content is discounted and rarely cited in AI answers.
pointers.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia ships a bylined expert-author programme with Person schema, credentials and sameAs.
pointers.recommendation: Tamazia ships a bylined expert-author programme with Person schema, credentials and sameAs.

bingo.problem: Right now: No bylined author or Person schema establishing expertise.
bingo.fix: Tamazia ships a bylined expert-author programme with Person schema, credentials and sameAs.

pointers.layman_explanation: llms.txt is the emerging standard that tells AI assistants how to represent your firm. Publishing one puts you ahead of peers who have not.

pointers.recommendation: Tamazia publishes a curated llms.txt.

pointers.citation: Spelling & grammar errors on your live site

pointers.layman_explanation: Visible errors on a professional firm's website quietly erode trust with high-value clients and signal low quality to Google's helpful-content systems. For a firm selling expertise, typos on the live site are an avoidable credibility leak.

pointers.recommendation: Tamazia proofreads and corrects every page, then sets an editorial QA gate so it does not recur.

pointers.fact: Homepage is missing a meta description.
pointers.layman_explanation: Search engines fall back to scraping random page text for your result snippet, which suppresses click-through. A deliberate description is a basic, high-leverage SEO control your site is missing.
pointers.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia writes sector-tuned meta descriptions across the site.
pointers.recommendation: Tamazia writes sector-tuned meta descriptions across the site.

bingo.problem: Right now: Homepage is missing a meta description.

pointers.fact: DMARC is set to p=none (monitor only, not enforcing).

pointers.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia moves you to p=quarantine then p=reject after monitoring.
pointers.recommendation: Tamazia moves you to p=quarantine then p=reject after monitoring.

bingo.problem: Right now: DMARC is set to p=none (monitor only, not enforcing).

pointers.fact: Your Domain Authority is 35/100. Top competitors: luxurylondon.co.uk 43/100, ibtimes.co.uk 40/100, fitnessfirst.co.uk 25/100.
pointers.layman_explanation: Domain Authority is the backlink-trust score search and AI engines use to decide who to rank and cite. This is where you stand against the firms competing for your buyers; staying ahead protects both your rankings and your AI citations.
pointers.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia runs the digital-PR and authority-building programme (earned links, citations, entity coverage) that defends and extends your Domain Authority lead.

pointers.fact: Your domain is not signed with DNSSEC.
pointers.layman_explanation: Without DNSSEC, attackers can forge DNS responses (cache poisoning) to silently redirect your visitors or intercept email. Enterprise, finance and government client security reviews increasingly require it.
pointers.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia enables DNSSEC at your DNS provider so every lookup of your domain is cryptographically verified.
pointers.recommendation: Tamazia enables DNSSEC at your DNS provider so every lookup of your domain is cryptographically verified.
pointers.evidence: DNS · no DS record and AD flag false

bingo.problem: Right now: Your domain is not signed with DNSSEC.

needs_review.fact: Cookie banner must offer a Reject-All / non-essential-refusal option as prominent and functional as Accept-All
needs_review.layman_explanation: Cookie banner must offer a Reject-All / non-essential-refusal option as prominent and functional as Accept-All. This is a specific requirement of UK PECR, which the ICO enforces; for a firm selling trust, its absence on the live site is an avoidable compliance and credibility breach.
needs_review.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia implements and verifies "Cookie banner must offer a Reject-All / non-essential-refusal option as prominent and func" on your site so it satisfies UK PECR.
needs_review.recommendation: Tamazia implements and verifies "Cookie banner must offer a Reject-All / non-essential-refusal option as prominent and func" on your site so it satisfies UK PECR.
needs_review.enforcement_example: DUAA 2025 raised the maximum PECR fine from 500000 to 17.5M or 4 percent of global turnover. The ICO is sweeping the top 1000 UK sites; in its first batch of 200, 134 firms were warned and given 30 days to fix non-compliant banners.

needs_review.layman_explanation: US visitors with disabilities can sue you for an inaccessible website. A published accessibility statement and WCAG 2.1 AA conformance shows good faith.
needs_review.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia ships the WCAG 2.1 AA accessibility statement and the conformance test report.
needs_review.recommendation: Tamazia ships the WCAG 2.1 AA accessibility statement and the conformance test report.
needs_review.enforcement_example: US Department of Justice and private suits under the ADA: injunctive relief, damages and attorney fees; web-accessibility suits are common.

needs_review.layman_explanation: If children are likely to use your service, the ICO Children Code requires age-appropriate design; not evident.
needs_review.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia applies the ICO Children Code standards.
needs_review.recommendation: Tamazia applies the ICO Children Code standards.
needs_review.enforcement_example: The ICO Children Code requires age-appropriate design for services likely accessed by children.

needs_review.layman_explanation: UK residents can request erasure; the route is not explained.
needs_review.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia adds a UK erasure route.
needs_review.recommendation: Tamazia adds a UK erasure route.
needs_review.enforcement_example: EU/UK GDPR Art. 17; fines up to GBP 17.5M or 4% turnover (ICO).

needs_review.layman_explanation: UK GDPR Art. 30 ROPA expected; none referenced.
needs_review.enforcement_example: EU/UK GDPR Art. 30; fines up to GBP 17.5M or 4% turnover (ICO).

needs_review.fact: Accessibility statement + WCAG 2.1 AA (ADA Title III)
needs_review.layman_explanation: ADA Title III expects WCAG 2.1 AA and an accessibility statement; missing.
needs_review.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia brings the site to WCAG 2.1 AA and adds an accessibility statement.
needs_review.recommendation: Tamazia brings the site to WCAG 2.1 AA and adds an accessibility statement.
needs_review.enforcement_example: DOJ 2024 guidance treats WCAG 2.1 AA as the ADA standard; thousands of Title III suits yearly.

needs_review.fact: "Limit the Use of My Sensitive Personal Information" link
needs_review.layman_explanation: CPRA requires a "Limit the Use of My Sensitive Personal Information" link where you use sensitive data; missing.
needs_review.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia adds the CPRA sensitive-data limit link.
needs_review.recommendation: Tamazia adds the CPRA sensitive-data limit link.
needs_review.enforcement_example: CPRA requires a "Limit the Use of My Sensitive Personal Information" link where sensitive data drives inference.

needs_review.fact: Organisation processing personal data with no sign of ICO data-protection-fee registration
needs_review.layman_explanation: Organisation processing personal data with no sign of ICO data-protection-fee registration. This is a specific requirement of the Data Protection Act 2018, which the ICO enforces; for a firm selling trust, its absence on the live site is an avoidable compliance and credibility breach.
needs_review.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia implements and verifies "Organisation processing personal data with no sign of ICO data-protection-fee registration" on your site so it satisfies the Data Protection Act 2018.
needs_review.recommendation: Tamazia implements and verifies "Organisation processing personal data with no sign of ICO data-protection-fee registration" on your site so it satisfies the Data Protection Act 2018.
needs_review.enforcement_example: Most UK organisations that process personal data must pay the ICO data protection fee of 40 to 2900 pounds and register. The ICO issues monetary penalties up to 4350 pounds for non-payment and publishes defaulters.

needs_review.layman_explanation: If turnover exceeds GBP 36M you must publish a Modern Slavery statement with a homepage link. Not found.
needs_review.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia publishes a compliant Modern Slavery statement and homepage link.
needs_review.recommendation: Tamazia publishes a compliant Modern Slavery statement and homepage link.
needs_review.enforcement_example: Modern Slavery Act s.54 requires a published statement for businesses with turnover GBP 36M+.

news_map.UK_CMA: CMA (2026-05-28): Court endorses CMA action as Emma Sleep agrees to change sales practices. Source: https://www.gov.uk/government/news/court-endorses-cma-action-as-emma-sleep-agrees-to-change-sales-practices
news_map.US_FTC: FTC (2026-05-21): FTC to Require Cox Media Group, Two Other Firms to Pay Nearly $1 Million to Settle Charges They Deceived Customers About “Active Listening” AI-Powered Marketing Service — $1M. Source: https://www.ftc.gov/news-events/news/press-releases/2026/05/ftc-require-cox-media-group-two-other-firms-pay-nearly-1-million-settle-charges-they-deceived
news_map.UK_GDPR_A13: ICO issued GBP 19.6M across 7 cases in 2025 (Capita GBP 14M, Advanced Computer Software GBP 3.07M, 23andMe GBP 2.31M, LastPass GBP 1.23M); two-thirds were UK GDPR breaches.
news_map.UK_DPA_2018: ICO 2025 enforcement hit GBP 19.6M from 7 cases (vs GBP 2.7M in 2024); the DUAA came into force 5 Feb 2026 with new compulsion powers.
news_map.US_FTC_ENDORSE: FTC (2026-05-21): FTC to Require Cox Media Group, Two Other Firms to Pay Nearly $1 Million to Settle Charges They Deceived Customers About “Active Listening” AI-Powered Marketing Service — $1M. Source: https://www.ftc.gov/news-events/news/press-releases/2026/05/ftc-require-cox-media-group-two-other-firms-pay-nearly-1-million-settle-charges-they-deceived
news_map.UK_PECR: The DUAA came into force 5 Feb 2026, raising the maximum PECR fine to GBP 17.5M (from GBP 500k); the ICO is reviewing the UK top 1,000 websites cookie banners.
news_map.UK_ICO_COOKIES: DUAA (in force 5 Feb 2026) lifts PECR fines to GBP 17.5M; the ICO is actively reviewing the top 1,000 UK sites and warned non-compliant cookie banners.
news_map.US_CCPA: California CPPA brought 12 enforcement actions in 2024, largest fine $1.55M.
news_map.US_ADA: DOJ ADA Title III digital-accessibility rule finalised April 2024. 4,000+ web-accessibility lawsuits in 2024.
news_map.UK_HSE: HSE prosecutions resulted in £55M of fines in 2024.
news_map.GOOGLE_EEAT: Google March 2024 core update emphasised E-E-A-T; sites without author bylines saw 31% traffic drop.
news_map.UK_DMCC_2024: CMA gained direct fining powers up to 10% global turnover from April 2025.
news_map.UK_COMPANIES_ACT: Companies House active enforcement of website disclosure post Economic Crime Act 2023.
news_map.US_TCPA: FCC AI-voice ruling Feb 2024. $500-$1,500 per call statutory damages.
news_map.US_CPRA: CPPA fined Honda $632,500 March 2025. First major CPRA enforcement post-DoorDash.
news_map.UK_EQUALITY_2010: EHRC 2024 digital-accessibility code. Damages claims up 18% in 2024.
news_map.UK_CRA_2015: CMA confirms CRA 2015 applies in parallel with DMCC. Cross-referenced in 2025 enforcement.

signals.title: Equinox Fitness Club - It's Not Fitness. It's Life.

audits.title: Speed Index
audits.description: Speed Index shows how quickly the contents of a page are visibly populated..

audits.title: Avoid multiple page redirects
audits.description: Redirects introduce additional delays before the page can be loaded..

audits.title: Time to Interactive
audits.description: Time to Interactive is the amount of time it takes for the page to become fully interactive..

audits.title: Largest Contentful Paint
audits.description: Largest Contentful Paint marks the time at which the largest text or image is painted.

audits.title: Avoid enormous network payloads
audits.description: Large network payloads cost users real money and are highly correlated with long load times..

audits.title: Improve image delivery
audits.description: Reducing the download time of images can improve the perceived load time of the page and LCP.

audits.title: Document request latency
audits.description: Your first network request is the most important. by avoiding redirects, ensuring a fast server response, and enabling text compression.

audits.title: Image elements do not have explicit `width` and `height`
audits.description: Set an explicit width and height on image elements to reduce layout shifts and improve CLS.

audits.title: Reduce unused JavaScript
audits.description: Reduce unused JavaScript and defer loading scripts until they are required to decrease bytes consumed by network activity..

audits.title: Background and foreground colors do not have a sufficient contrast ratio.
audits.description: Low-contrast text is difficult or impossible for many users to read..

audits.title: First Contentful Paint
audits.description: First Contentful Paint marks the time at which the first text or image is painted..

audits.title: Render-blocking requests
audits.description: Requests are blocking the page's initial render, which may delay LCP. can move these network requests out of the critical path.

audits.title: Use efficient cache lifetimes
audits.description: A long cache lifetime can speed up repeat visits to your page..

audits.title: Legacy JavaScript
audits.description: Polyfills and transforms enable older browsers to use new JavaScript features. However, many aren't necessary for modern browsers. Consider modifying your JavaScript build process to not transpile features, unless you know you must support older browsers.

audits.title: Reduce unused CSS
audits.description: Reduce unused rules from stylesheets and defer CSS not used for above-the-fold content to decrease bytes consumed by network activity..

audits.title: Network dependency tree
audits.description: by reducing the length of chains, reducing the download size of resources, or deferring the download of unnecessary resources to improve page load.

regimes.why: applies to every site that wants to rank in Google or be cited by AI answer engines, regardless of where it is registered

jurisdiction_statement.statement: equinox is registered in the United States and its own website shows it serves clients in the US. It is therefore bound by US law (CCPA/CPRA and ~20 state privacy laws, FTC, ADA), not only the law of its country of registration. This audit applies the laws of every jurisdiction the site shows you operate in, and no others.

terms.domain authority: A 0 to 100 score of how much trust your site has earned from links on other websites. Higher means Google and AI engines are more likely to rank and cite you. It is the single biggest reason one site outranks another.
terms.da: Domain Authority: a 0 to 100 trust score based on the quality of sites linking to you. The higher it is, the easier everything else ranks.
terms.pa: Page Authority: the same trust idea as Domain Authority but for one specific page rather than the whole site.
terms.geo: Generative Engine Optimisation: whether AI answer engines (ChatGPT, Gemini, Perplexity, Google AI Overviews) actually name and cite your business when a buyer asks them for a provider like you.
terms.ai visibility: Whether the AI answer engines buyers now ask first will name and cite you, or only your competitors.
terms.schema: Hidden code (structured data) that tells search engines and AI exactly who you are, what you offer and where. Without it they have to guess, and they guess wrong.
terms.structured data: Hidden code that spells out who you are and what you offer so Google and AI can read it precisely instead of guessing.
terms.llms.txt: A simple file that tells AI models what your site is about and what they may use. It is a new standard and most firms do not have one yet, so adding it is an easy edge.
terms.ai crawler: The automated readers (GPTBot, ClaudeBot, PerplexityBot, Google-Extended) that AI answer engines send to read your site. Block them and those engines cannot cite you.
terms.robots.txt: A file that tells web crawlers which parts of your site they may read. One wrong line here can make you invisible to Google or to AI engines.
terms.nap: Name, Address, Phone. Search engines cross-check these to trust that your business is real and local. Inconsistent or missing NAP sinks local rankings.
terms.local pack: The three businesses Google shows on a small map at the top of local searches. Most local clicks go to those three, so being outside it costs real enquiries.
terms.map pack: The three-business map result at the top of local searches. The top spots take the majority of local clicks.
terms.e-e-a-t: Google's test of Experience, Expertise, Authoritativeness and Trust. It decides whether your content deserves to rank, and it matters most in law, health and finance.
terms.gdpr: The data-protection law for the UK and EU. Fines reach GBP 17.5M or 4% of global turnover, so gaps here are the most expensive on the page.
terms.uk gdpr: The UK version of the EU data-protection law, enforced by the ICO with fines up to GBP 17.5M or 4% of global turnover.
terms.pecr: UK rules on cookies and electronic marketing. You must get clear consent before non-essential cookies or trackers fire.
terms.ccpa: California's privacy law, and the template for the roughly 20 US states that now have one. It gives consumers the right to opt out and to have data deleted.
terms.canonical: A line of code that tells Google which version of a page is the real one, so duplicate web addresses do not split your ranking power.
terms.meta description: The summary Google shows under your result. A good one lifts click-through; if it is missing, Google writes its own from random page text.
terms.hsts: A security header that stops your site being downgraded from secure HTTPS. Corporate buyers check for it, so its absence is an easy red flag.
terms.csp: Content-Security-Policy: a security header that blocks malicious scripts. Security reviews check for it.
terms.lcp: Largest Contentful Paint: how long your main content takes to appear. Over 2.5 seconds and Google ranks you lower and visitors leave.
terms.cls: Cumulative Layout Shift: how much your page jumps around while loading. Jumpy pages frustrate users and rank worse.
terms.sameas: Schema links that connect your site to your verified profiles (LinkedIn, Companies House, Wikidata) so AI knows the business is really you.
terms.wikidata: Google's and AI's open knowledge base. An entry there makes you a recognised entity that answer engines can confidently cite.
terms.share of voice: How often you appear versus your competitors when buyers search or ask AI in your category. Low share of voice means they are heard and you are not.
terms.organic: The unpaid search results, as opposed to ads. Ranking here is the durable, compounding kind of visibility.
